1: Μετὰ ταῦτα — the Evangelist's characteristic connective (meta tauta, after these things) marks an indefinite time-gap. Unlike the Synoptic Gospels, which sequence the feeding directly after other events, John leaves the interval undefined. The Galilean Sea is identified by both its common name and the newer Roman designation "Sea of Tiberias" — a parenthetical clarification for non-Palestinian readers that functions as a geographic authenticating detail.
2: ὄχλος πολύς — "great crowd" is the Evangelist's term for the mass of Galilean followers who are not yet disciples in any committed sense. Their motive is stated explicitly: oti heōrōn ta sēmeia — because they were watching the signs he was doing on the sick. The imperfect heōrōn (were watching, ongoing) indicates sustained spectator attention rather than faith-response; this crowd follows because of what they see, not because they understand what it signifies.
3: ἀνῆλθεν εἰς τὸ ὄρος Ἰησοῦς — the verb anabainō (to go up) appears throughout John with both literal and symbolic weight. The mountain here echoes the Sinai tradition: Moses received the Law on a mountain; the Bread of Life discourse will explicitly contrast the manna Moses mediated with the bread the Father gives. The Evangelist does not develop the mountain typology explicitly, but the spatial positioning is deliberate.
4: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς τὸ πάσχα — the Evangelist timestamps the chapter at Passover proximity. This is John's second Passover in the Gospel (cf. 2:13; a third will come at ch.12–19). The Passover setting activates a set of associations: the Exodus feeding narrative, the Passover lamb, the bread eaten in haste. The discourse that follows will engage the manna tradition directly.
5: πόθεν ἀγοράσωμεν ἄρτους — the deliberative subjunctive agorasōmen (shall we buy?) treats the feeding problem as a purchasing question. Agorazō (G59) means to market-purchase; the word choice grounds the problem in ordinary commerce. The question is addressed to Philip specifically, though all the disciples are present — perhaps because Philip was from nearby Bethsaida (1:44) and would know the local supply chains.
6: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγεν πειράζων αὐτόν — the Evangelist's parenthetical clarification is unusual: he breaks the narrative to explain Jesus's interior motive. Peirazō (G3985) means to test or put to proof, not to tempt toward sin; the word carries the sense of pedagogical examination. Jesus already knew what he would do (ēdei gar ti emellen poiein) — the question to Philip was diagnostic, not information-seeking. This is the Evangelist's way of marking miraculous episodes as planned revelatory acts, not improvised responses.
7: διακοσίων δηναρίων ἄρτοι — 200 denarii was approximately 200 days' wages for a day laborer. Philip's calculation is not pessimistic exaggeration; it is realistic arithmetic. The detail establishes that the problem is humanly insoluble — the crowd cannot be fed by normal means. This impossibility is the precondition for the sign.
8: Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου — Andrew is consistently identified in the Fourth Gospel in relation to his brother Peter (cf. 1:40). His contribution to the dialogue is a resource-count that also concludes its own insufficiency: "but what are these among so many?" (alla tauta ti estin eis tosoutous). The adversative alla signals that Andrew himself knows the answer is inadequate.
9:
παιδάριον ἓν ὃ ἔχει κριθίνους ἄρτους πέντε — the barley loaves (krithinoi artoi) are significant: barley bread was the food of the poor, a fraction the cost of wheat bread. The Elisha parallel (2 Kgs 4:42–44) is exact — a man brought barley loaves to Elisha; Elisha fed a hundred with twenty loaves and had left over. John does not cite the connection, but the coincidence of grain type, the feeding of a crowd, and the remainder all mark the allusion for readers of the LXX.

καὶ δύο ὀψάρια — opsarion (G3795, diminutive) means small fish or fish-relish — the kind of salted fish used as condiment with bread, not a substantial portion. The emphasis is on inadequacy: what a small boy carries for a meal cannot feed five thousand.
10: ἀνέπεσαν οὖν οἱ ἄνδρες — anepeson (reclined, aorist of anapiptō) is the symposium or formal-meal posture, used throughout the Gospels for dining. Andres (men, males) is specifically enumerated at five thousand — a detail that survives in all four Gospels. Women and children, if present (Matt 14:21), are not counted. The grass detail (ēn de chortos polys) serves as another authenticating eyewitness note and may recall Ps 23:2 ("He makes me lie down in green pastures").
11: λαβὼν οὖν τοὺς ἄρτους ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εὐχαριστήσας — the verb eucharistēsas (aorist participle of eucharisteō, G2168) — "having given thanks" — is the root of the later Christian term "Eucharist." The giving-of-thanks formula corresponds to the Jewish berakhah over bread. John uses eucharisteō here (and at v.23 when he mentions "the place where they ate bread after the Lord had given thanks"); the Synoptics use both eucharisteō and eulogeō (blessed). διένειμεν — he distributed; the imperfect dietithou in Luke marks the ongoing distribution; John's aorist takes it as a completed act. Hosou ēthelon — as much as they wished: the supply was not rationed.
12: συναγάγετε τὰ περισσεύσαντα κλάσματα — the command to gather (synagagete, aorist imperative) and the reason given — hina mē ti apōlētai (that nothing may be lost) — is notable. The verb apollymi (perish, be lost) is the same word used in 3:16 ("should not perish") and 6:39 ("I should lose none"). The Evangelist may be planting a lexical connection: Jesus loses none of the bread given to him, just as he loses none of those the Father gives him.
13: δώδεκα κοφίνους κλασμάτων — twelve baskets (kophinos = a large wicker basket, the type associated with Jewish travelers in the ancient world) filled with fragments from five barley loaves. The number twelve exceeds the starting supply by multiples. Whether the twelve baskets correspond symbolically to the twelve tribes of Israel or the twelve disciples, the Evangelist does not say; the number stands as numeric surplus impossible to attribute to waste recovery.
14: οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ προφήτης ὁ ἐρχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "the Prophet" (ho prophētēs with the definite article) refers to the Mosaic prophet of Deut 18:15–18, whom Israel expected at the end of days ("the LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me"). The Evangelist has John the Baptist deny this identity at 1:21 ("Are you the Prophet? He said, No"). Now the crowd assigns it to Jesus on the basis of the feeding sign — Moses fed Israel in the wilderness with manna; this man has fed multitudes with bread. The sign is correctly read as prophetic; the conclusion is not entirely wrong, only insufficient.
15: ἁρπάζειν αὐτὸν ἵνα ποιήσωσιν βασιλέα — harpazein (G726, to seize, snatch, take by force) is a violent word — used for robbery, abduction, and forcible seizure. The crowd's intent is to install Jesus as king by force (harpazein... poiēsōsin basilea). Jesus perceives this (gnous, having known) and withdraws. The response shows the gulf between the crowd's messianic category (nationalist king who solves food scarcity) and what Jesus offers. ἀνεχώρησεν πάλιν εἰς τὸ ὄρος — he withdrew again to the mountain; autos monos (himself alone) emphasizes solitary retreat from the crowd's pressure.
16: Ὡς δὲ ὀψία ἐγένετο — the temporal marker "when evening came" (opsia, late afternoon) signals the disciples' descent to the sea without Jesus. The two movements in this section — crowd below, Jesus on the mountain alone, disciples on the sea in darkness — set the spatial and moral contrasts for the epiphany that follows.
17: σκοτία ἤδη ἐγεγόνει — "darkness had already come" — the pluperfect egegonei marks the darkness as an established condition at the time of the disciples' embarkation. The Evangelist's use of skotia (G4653, darkness) is rarely incidental; throughout the Gospel the term carries the register of spiritual opposition to the light (1:5; 3:19; 12:35). Here the physical darkness of night is the setting, but the language activates the broader associations.
18: ἥ τε θάλασσα ἀνέμου μεγάλου πνέοντος διεγείρετο — the sea was being aroused (diegeireto, imperfect passive) by a strong wind blowing (anemou megalou pneontos, genitive absolute). The passive suggests an external force agitating the water. Pneuma (G4151, wind/spirit, dl=3) in its literal register here; but the Evangelist's pneuma vocabulary is wide. Nothing in the grammar signals double meaning, but readers of the prologue will note the term.
19: σταδίους εἴκοσι πέντε ἢ τριάκοντα — 25 to 30 stadia is approximately 3–4 miles, which corresponds to the midpoint of the crossing, consistent with Josephus's estimate of the lake's width at 40 stadia. The imprecision ("25 or 30") reads as eyewitness memory rather than schematic number. θεωροῦσιν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περιπατοῦντα ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης — theōrousin (present: they are seeing, ongoing perception) and peripatōn (walking, present participle: continuous walking on the sea). The terror (ephobēthēsan, they were frightened) is stated without elaboration.
20: ἐγώ εἰμι· μὴ φοβεῖσθε — the bare egō eimi without predicate (I AM; not "it is I" in the Greek) resonates with the Septuagintal rendering of the divine name in Isa 43:10, 25; 51:12; Exod 3:14. The syntactic form can be read as simple self-identification ("It is I") — which is how the disciples receive it — but the Evangelist deploys the phrase at crisis moments where divine identity is at stake. The attached command mē phobeisthe (stop being afraid, present imperative of prohibition) echoes the theophany formula: "Fear not, I AM" appears in divine-appearance contexts throughout the LXX (Gen 15:1; 26:24; Isa 41:10).
21: εὐθέως ἐγένετο τὸ πλοῖον ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς — "immediately the boat was at the land" (eutheōs egeneto). This compression — the boat instantly arrives at its destination — is handled as a matter of fact without elaboration. Whether it is a second miracle (instantaneous transport) or a compressed narrative description of a rapid completion of the voyage is left unresolved. The Evangelist does not signal which, and the Greek permits either reading.
22: The extended narrative of vv.22–24 tracks the crowd's realization that Jesus has left: only one boat was there, Jesus did not board it, yet he is gone. The logical impossibility leads them across the lake to Capernaum. The detail that other boats came from Tiberias (ek Tiberiados ploia) is incidental authentication of how the crowd was able to follow.
23: ἄλλα ἦλθεν πλοιάρια ἐκ Τιβεριάδος — a parenthetical geographic note. Ploiaria (diminutive, small boats) from Tiberias — the new Herodian city on the western shore — arrived near the feeding site. The parenthetical phrase "near the place where they ate the bread after the Lord had given thanks" reuses eucharistēsantos tou Kyriou — the Evangelist twice uses the eucharist-vocabulary in connection with the feeding (vv.11 and 23).
24: The crowd's search for Jesus is driven by the same motive as their initial following (v.2): sign-and-bread interest. The Evangelist uses zētountes ton Iēsoun (seeking Jesus) — the verb zēteō in John often marks problematic searching, motivated by self-interest or hostile intent, in contrast to the coming (erchomai) that characterizes genuine belief.
25: Ῥαββί, πότε ὧδε γέγονας — "Rabbi, when did you get here?" The perfect gegonas (have you come to be/arrived) treats the question as about his achieved presence. They address him as Rabbi (teacher), the appropriate honorific for a miracle-worker in their understanding. Jesus does not answer the question — he addresses the motive beneath it.
26: ζητεῖτέ με οὐχ ὅτι εἴδετε σημεῖα — "you seek me not because you saw signs." The aorist eidete (you saw) is contrasted with what they actually experienced: hoti ephagete ek tōn artōn — "because you ate from the loaves." The Evangelist's critique is precise: they saw the sign-event but did not perceive it as a sign pointing beyond itself. Sēmeion (G4592) in John always means a significant act that reveals something about the actor and calls for faith-response; they experienced only the bread. Echortasthēte — you were satisfied/filled to capacity (from chortazō, to feed until full, used also of animals grazing).
27:
ἐργάζεσθε μὴ τὴν βρῶσιν τὴν ἀπολλυμένην — ergazesthe (work, present imperative) picks up the labor vocabulary. Brōsis (G1035, food/the act of eating) rather than artos (bread); the term is broader. Apollymenēn (present participle: perishing, being destroyed) — the same root as apollymi in 3:16. Two categories of food are named: perishable food and food that endures (menousan) to eternal life.

αἰώνιον (G166, aiōnios, dl=3) — rendered "eternal" throughout this chapter. The dispute is whether the word is primarily temporal (everlasting, without end) or qualitative (belonging to the age to come, eschatological life of a different order). The Johannine context supports both: eternal life is the life that does not end, but it begins now (6:47: "the one who believes has eternal life," present tense). σφραγίζω — "the Father has sealed him" (sfragizō, aorist indicative): to set a seal on is to authenticate, authorize, confirm as genuine. God's seal on the Son is the divine endorsement of his mission and identity.
28: τί ποιῶμεν ἵνα ἐργαζώμεθα τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ — the crowd's question rephrases Jesus's exhortation using the plural erga (works, deeds). They hear a program of religious activity. The plural is important: they imagine a list of qualifying actions. Jesus will answer with the singular.
29: τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ ἔργον τοῦ θεοῦ — "this is the work of God" — singular ergon. The plural request (works) is met with a single work. Hina pisteuēte eis hon apesteilen ekeinos — "that you believe in the one he has sent." Pisteuō eis (believe into/toward) is the directional construction John uses for salvific faith — not merely belief-that but trust directed toward a person. All religious doing is sublimated into one posture: believing into the Sent One.
30: τί οὖν ποιεῖς σὺ σημεῖον — the crowd demands a sign to justify belief, despite having just witnessed the feeding. The question reveals their interpretive framework: one spectacular feat is not enough; Moses gave sustained daily manna for forty years. Their implicit calculus is that Jesus's credentials require matching or exceeding Moses's sign. The irony is that they are asking for a sign while standing in the aftermath of one.
31: τοὺς πατέρας ἡμῶν τὸ μάννα ἔφαγον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ — the citation ("He gave them bread from heaven to eat") blends Ps 78:24 and Exod 16:4. The crowd ascribes the giving to Moses: kathōs estin gegrammeron — "as it is written." Jesus will correct this in v.32: Moses did not give; the Father gives. The Exodus manna tradition serves as the paradigm the crowd applies to evaluate Jesus, and which Jesus will reframe and surpass.
32: οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — the emphatic negation corrects both the subject and the tense. (1) Subject: not Moses but my Father (ho patēr mou) gives. Moses was instrument; the Father was giver — even for the manna. (2) Tense: edōken (he gave, aorist — past, the crowd's framing) vs. didōsin (he gives, present — ongoing now). The shift from aorist to present is the exegetical move: the true bread from heaven is not a historical provision that ended; it is a present gift.
33: ὁ γὰρ ἄρτος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν ὁ καταβαίνων ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — "the bread of God is the one coming down from heaven." The present participle katabainōn (coming down, ongoing descent) identifies the bread as a person — "and giving life to the world" (zōēn didous tō kosmō). Kosmos (the world) marks the scope of the life-giving: not Israel alone, not the wilderness generation, but the entire human world. This is the bread that surpasses manna.
34: κύριε, πάντοτε δὸς ἡμῖν τὸν ἄρτον τοῦτον — the request parallels the Samaritan woman's request at 4:15: "Sir, give me this water always." Both speakers understand the referent literally and want a permanent solution to physical need. The irony in both cases is that they are requesting the very person they are addressing, without realizing it. Pantote (always) intensifies the request: a sustained supply, like the manna that fell every morning.
35: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς — the first fully articulated I AM with predicate in this chapter. The double construction: ho erchomenos pros me ou mē peinasē (the one coming to me will certainly not hunger — double negative ou mē with aorist subjunctive, emphatic futurity) and ho pisteuōn eis eme ou mē dipsēsei pōpote (the one believing in me will certainly never thirst). Coming and believing are presented as parallel acts: both are directional orientation toward Jesus. The hunger/thirst imagery recasts physical appetite as the proper image of spiritual need — not a metaphor chosen arbitrarily but one that activates the entire discourse about bread that began with the feeding miracle.
36: ἀλλ᾽ εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι καὶ ἑωράκατέ με καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε — "you have seen me and yet you do not believe." The perfect heōrakate (you have seen and the result stands) followed by the present ou pisteuete (you are not believing). The contrast between seeing and believing runs through the Gospel (cf. 20:29). The crowd has seen both Jesus and his signs; their unbelief is therefore not due to ignorance of evidence but to something else — which Jesus explains in vv.37–44.
37: πᾶν ὃ δίδωσίν μοι ὁ πατὴρ πρὸς ἐμὲ ἥξει — "all that the Father gives me will come to me." The pronoun pan ho (all that) is neuter singular — a collective construction treating those given as a unified whole (contrast pantes hoi, all the ones). The coming is stated as certainty: hēxei (future indicative, will come). The following clause balances this with an equally certain reception: ton erchomenon pros me ou mē ekbalō exō — "the one coming to me I will certainly not drive out" (double negative again, aorist subjunctive). Divine initiative in giving and human act of coming are held together without resolving their relationship.
38: ὅτι καταβέβηκα ἀπὸ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — the perfect katabebēka (I have come down and the result stands, I am here as one who has come down). The descent is a completed event with present consequence. The purpose stated is double: ouch hina poiō to thelēma to emon (not to do my own will) — alla to thelēma tou pempsantos me (but the will of the one who sent me). The mission of the Son is defined by the Sender's will, not independent agenda.
39: ἵνα πᾶν ὃ δέδωκέν μοι μὴ ἀπολέσω ἐξ αὐτοῦ — "that of all he has given me I should lose none." The verb apollymi (lose, destroy) reappears: the Father's will is that the Son lose nothing of what the Father has given. Anastēsō autō en tē eschatē hēmera — "I will raise it up at the last day" (eschatē hēmera). This is the first of four appearances of "the last day" in this chapter (vv.39,40,44,54). The phrase is Johannine — John uses it for the day of resurrection and judgment where the other Gospel writers use "that day" or "the age to come." The future tense resurrection-promise is the guarantee of non-loss.
40: πᾶς ὁ θεωρῶν τὸν υἱόν — theōrōn (present participle of theōreō, to behold, observe attentively) is stronger than mere seeing (horaō); it involves sustained, contemplative attention. Kai pisteuōn eis auton — and believing in him (the second present participle, simultaneous action). The two present participles describe the continuous posture of the believer: ongoing beholding and ongoing believing. The consequence: echē zōēn aiōnion (has eternal life, present tense — already in possession) and anastēsō auton egō tē eschatē hēmera (I myself will raise him up at the last day, emphatic egō).
41: ἐγόγγυζον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι περὶ αὐτοῦ — egongyzon (imperfect of gongyzon) is the LXX term for Israel's wilderness murmuring — used in Exod 16 and Num 11 for Israel's complaints against Moses and God over food. The Evangelist's choice of this word activates the typological parallel deliberately: the crowd who wanted more bread (v.34) now grumbles, just as Israel grumbled for bread in the wilderness. The irony is sharp — those who are supposed to receive the bread from heaven are murmuring against the one who is the bread from heaven.
42: οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν Ἰησοῦς ὁ υἱὸς Ἰωσήφ — the objection is genealogical: they know his father and mother. The familiarity functions as a disqualification: how can a man whose parents are known claim to have come down from heaven? This is the scandal of the Incarnation stated from the outside. The Evangelist does not answer the objection directly — Jesus responds by redirecting attention from origin to invitation and the Father's drawing activity.
43: μὴ γογγύζετε μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων — "stop murmuring among yourselves" (present imperative of prohibition = stop an action in progress). The command uses the same gongyzo word (now the imperative) and by doing so explicitly identifies their behavior as the wilderness-murmuring pattern. The reproach is implicit: they are acting like Israel in the desert, who was judged for that murmuring.
44: οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ὁ πατὴρ ὁ πέμψας με ἑλκύσῃ αὐτόν — "No one is able to come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him." Helkysē (aorist subjunctive of helkō, G1670) means to draw or drag — the word is used literally in 21:6,11 for dragging fish nets, and in 12:32 where Jesus says "when I am lifted up I will draw all to myself." The force of helkō is more than mere invitation; it carries the idea of active, effective pulling. This verse grounds the failure to believe (v.36) and the certainty of those who do come (v.37) in the Father's initiative. The theological weight of the verse is sustained; the Evangelist does not soften it.
45: ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τοῖς προφήταις — "It stands written in the Prophets" (esti gegramenon, perfect passive — a fixed, standing text). The citation is from Isa 54:13 LXX: kai esontai pantes didaktoi theou (they shall all be taught by God). Jesus applies a restoration promise made to future Israel to the present gathering of disciples. πᾶς ὁ ἀκούσας παρὰ τοῦ πατρός — "everyone who has heard from the Father and learned" comes to Jesus. The sequence matters: hearing is prior to coming; the Father's instruction is what issues in movement toward the Son.
46: οὐχ ὅτι τὸν πατέρα ἑώρακέν τις εἰ μὴ ὁ ὢν παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ — v.45 might suggest the crowd has equal access to the Father; v.46 corrects this. No one has seen the Father — the perfect heōraken (has seen, ever seen) rules it out universally — except the one who is from God. Ho ōn para tou theou (the one who is from God, present participle of being) uses the same ōn that appeared in 1:18 ("the one who is in the bosom of the Father"). Only the Son is in that position of vision; all teaching about the Father that reaches humans comes through him.
47: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν — the double amen formula is unique to the Fourth Gospel (the Synoptics use single amen). It functions as an authoritative preamble: "I tell you with solemn guarantee." ὁ πιστεύων ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "the one who believes has eternal life." The present tense echei (has, possesses now) is emphatic. This is John's characteristic realized eschatology: eternal life is not merely future but a present possession of the believer. The tense cannot be collapsed into future expectation without distorting the grammar.
48: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς — the I AM declaration from v.35 is repeated now at the center of the discourse, after the theological exposition of vv.36–47. The repetition is not redundant; it functions as a refrain that anchors the doctrinal argument. The discourse began with a crowd wanting bread; it arrives here at the identification of Jesus himself as that bread. What they requested at v.34 (give us this bread always) he now re-identifies as himself.
49: οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν ἔφαγον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ τὸ μάννα καὶ ἀπέθανον — "your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness and died." This is the fourth sustained reference to the manna narrative in the discourse (after vv.31, 32, 38, 49). The datum is stated bluntly: even those who ate the miraculous divine provision died. The manna was a sign of grace but not the source of life that transcends physical death. The contrast with v.50 is sharpened by this acknowledgment of manna's limitation.
50: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβαίνων ἵνα τις ἐξ αὐτοῦ φάγῃ καὶ μὴ ἀποθάνῃ — the purpose clause hina tis... phagē kai mē apothanē (that one may eat and not die) states what the manna could not provide. Phagē is aorist subjunctive of phagō (to eat), the ordinary word; the shift to trōgō (more physically specific) comes in v.54. The comparison with v.49 is direct: those who ate manna died; those who eat this bread will not die.
51: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς — the third I AM of the chapter, now with the expanded predicate: not "the bread of life" but "the living bread" (ho artos ho zōn, the bread that is itself alive, living). The participle zōn (living, present) distinguishes this from merely bread that produces life; this bread has life in itself. ἡ σάρξ μου ἐστίν — "this bread is my flesh." Sarx (G4561, dl=4) here denotes the full physical human body — the flesh in which the incarnate Word is present — not sinful human nature. The word choice is as maximal as possible: not body (sōma), not self (psychē), but flesh — the most concrete, physical designation. Hyper tēs tou kosmou zōēs — "for the life of the world": the giving is substitutionary in direction (for, on behalf of) and universal in scope (the world).
52: ἐμάχοντο οὖν πρὸς ἀλλήλους — emachonto (imperfect of machomai, to fight, quarrel, strive) indicates an ongoing heated dispute, not a momentary confusion. The argument is conducted within the crowd: "How can this man give us his flesh to eat?" The question takes the claim literally and finds it offensive, even abhorrent. Jesus does not clarify that he means something metaphorical — he intensifies the claim in v.53.
53: ἐὰν μὴ φάγητε τὴν σάρκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ πίητε αὐτοῦ τὸ αἷμα — the condition is double: eating the flesh of the Son of Man AND drinking his blood. Sarx (dl=4) again, now paired with haima (blood, G129) — two substances that are maximally physical and maximally associated with sacrificial death. Jewish law strictly prohibited the consumption of blood (Lev 17:14; Gen 9:4); the demand to drink blood would have been heard as shocking in the extreme. Jesus does not withdraw the claim; he escalates it. Zōēn ouk echete en heautois — "you have no life in yourselves." The consequence of non-eating is stated negatively: the absence of life.
54: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα — from this verse through v.58, the verb for eating shifts from phagō/esthiō to trōgō (G5176). Trōgō carries the sense of munching, gnawing, chewing — a more concrete, physical action than the generic eating verbs. The shift is not accidental; it reinforces the physical reality of the flesh being spoken of against any purely spiritualizing interpretation. Whether this applies primarily to the Eucharist, to appropriating Christ by faith, or to both is a question the Evangelist does not close. What he does rule out is a reading that treats the flesh and blood as mere metaphor with no bodily referent. κἀγὼ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ — the promise of resurrection at the last day appears again (third occurrence in the chapter), now attached specifically to the one who eats and drinks.
55: ἡ γὰρ σάρξ μου ἀληθής ἐστιν βρῶσις καὶ τὸ αἷμά μου ἀληθής ἐστιν πόσις — "my flesh is real food and my blood is real drink." The adjective alēthēs (true, real, genuine, G227) distinguishes this food from any substitute or symbol. Some manuscripts read alēthōs (truly, adverb) — either reading makes the same assertion. The grammar declares that the flesh and blood constitute actual, not figurative, nourishment — whatever the precise mode of that nourishment is.
56: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μένει κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ — the consequence of eating and drinking is mutual indwelling: en emoi menei kagō en autō (remains in me and I in him). The verb menō (G3306, abide/remain/dwell) is John's characteristic term for the union between believer and Christ, and between Christ and the Father (15:4–10). The flesh-and-blood language opens into the menō language: physical eating produces ontological union. The structure parallels the vine and branches metaphor of ch.15.
57: καθὼς ἀπέστειλέν με ὁ ζῶν πατὴρ κἀγὼ ζῶ διὰ τὸν πατέρα — "just as the living Father sent me and I live because of the Father." The kathōs... kagō (just as... so also I) construction creates an analogy: the Son's relation of life-from-the-Father is the model for the believer's relation of life-from-the-Son. Dia ton patera (because of the Father, literally "through the Father") — the Son's life is derived, not self-generated; similarly ho trōgōn me kakeinos zēsetai di᾽ eme — "the one who feeds on me will live because of me." The chain of derived life descends: Father → Son → believer.
58: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς, οὐ καθὼς ἔφαγον οἱ πατέρες καὶ ἀπέθανον — the manna contrast recurs for the sixth and final time, now as the discourse's closing summary. The definitive comparison: "not as the fathers ate and died." The discourse began with the crowd citing manna (v.31); it closes with Jesus re-citing the manna only to mark the final contrast. The repetition throughout has been building the argument: the manna was genuine provision, genuinely from God, but it could not give eternal life. This bread can and does.
59: Ταῦτα εἶπεν ἐν συναγωγῇ διδάσκων ἐν Καφαρναούμ — the Evangelist closes the discourse with a setting notation: he said these things while teaching in the synagogue at Capernaum. The synagogue setting is significant: this is not private instruction to disciples but public teaching in the principal institution of Jewish communal life. The audience was therefore wider and more institutionally Jewish than the lakeside crowd; the offense of the flesh-and-blood teaching was delivered in a formal religious context.
60: Σκληρός ἐστιν ὁ λόγος οὗτος — "This is a hard saying." Sklēros (G4642) means hard, rough, harsh, offensive — not merely difficult to understand but objectionable, unacceptable to those hearing it. The complaint comes not from the crowd but from polloi ek tōn mathētōn autou (many of his disciples) — those who had been following him. Tis dynatai autou akouein — "who can accept/hear it?" The verb akouō (G191) here means to receive, to accept, not merely to hear with the ear.
61: εἰδὼς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν ἑαυτῷ ὅτι γογγύζουσιν — "knowing within himself that they were grumbling." The participle eidōs (knowing, from oida) indicates divine inner knowledge — Jesus knows without being told. The wilderness-murmuring vocabulary (gongyzosin) returns, now applied to his own disciples. The word choice is deliberate and withering: not just the hostile crowd but his followers are behaving like Israel in the desert.
62: ἐὰν οὖν θεωρῆτε τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἀναβαίνοντα ὅπου ἦν τὸ πρότερον — "what then if you see the Son of Man ascending where he was before?" The question is conditional and rhetorical. Anabainonta (ascending, present participle) — the verb anabainō that opened the chapter (Jesus went up the mountain, v.3) now points to the eschatological ascent. Hopou ēn to proteron — "where he was before" — pre-existence is asserted: the Son of Man has a prior heavenly location. The implied argument: if you stumble at incarnate flesh, what will you do when you see him return whence he came? The offense of the Incarnation is not resolved by the Ascension; if anything, the Ascension confirms the entire claim.
63: τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν τὸ ζῳοποιοῦν, ἡ σὰρξ οὐκ ὠφελεῖ οὐδέν — "the Spirit is the one giving life; the flesh profits nothing." Pneuma (G4151, dl=3) and sarx (G4561, dl=4) stand in antithesis. The verse has been read as a qualification of vv.51-58: if "flesh profits nothing," perhaps the flesh-and-blood language there was purely metaphorical. But this reading misunderstands the antithesis. The sarx that profits nothing here is not the flesh of Christ spoken of in the discourse; it is the natural human capacity to understand spiritual realities apart from divine revelation. Flesh = unregenerate human perception; Spirit = the divine capacity that gives life. τὰ ῥήματά μου πνεῦμά ἐστιν καὶ ζωή ἐστιν — "the words I have spoken are spirit and are life." Rhēmata (spoken utterances, G4487) rather than logos (word); Jesus refers to the specific discourse just delivered. These utterances are characterized as pneuma and zōē — they are received through the Spirit and they communicate life.
64: ἀλλ᾽ εἰσὶν ἐξ ὑμῶν τινες οἳ οὐ πιστεύουσιν — "but there are some of you who do not believe." The Evangelist adds his parenthetical note: ēdei gar ἐξ ἀρχῆς ὁ Ἰησοῦς τίνες εἰσὶν οἱ μὴ πιστεύοντες — "for Jesus had known from the beginning which ones were not believing" (present participle). The foreknowledge of Jesus encompasses both unbelief and betrayal: kai tis estin ho paradōsōn auton (and who the one about to betray him is). The future participle paradōsōn points to Judas, identified at v.71.
65: οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ᾖ δεδομένον αὐτῷ ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — the statement of v.44 is restated in different form: "no one can come to me unless it has been given to him from the Father." The passive periphrastic ē dedomenon (it has been given and stands given) places the enabling firmly on the divine side. The discourse began with the Father giving the Son bread to distribute; it ends with the Father giving certain people to the Son. Both the gift of bread and the gift of persons are divine initiatives.
66: ἐκ τούτου πολλοὶ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω — "from this time many of his disciples went away to the things behind." Eis ta opisō (to the things behind) is the expression for reverting: they turned back, retreated from following. Ouketi met᾽ autou periepatoun — "no longer walked with him." The verb peripateō (to walk with, G4043) is the term used throughout the Gospels for discipleship as accompanying. They stopped accompanying him. The mass departure is not explained or lamented by the Evangelist; it is stated as the consequence of the teaching.
67: μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε ὑπάγειν — "You do not also want to go away, do you?" The particle mē before the question expects the answer "no" — but the question is genuinely open. The Twelve are addressed as a group. Hypagein (to go away, depart, withdraw) uses the same verb that will later describe Judas's departure (13:3). The question does not deny that they could leave; it invites their own declaration.
68: κύριε, πρὸς τίνα ἀπελευσόμεθα; ῥήματα ζωῆς αἰωνίου ἔχεις — Simon Peter's response is not a statement of unqualified understanding but of exclusive commitment: "Lord, to whom shall we go? You have words of eternal life." Rhēmata — the spoken utterances, picking up the same word from v.63 where Jesus called his words "spirit and life." Peter does not say he understood the bread-and-blood discourse; he says that Jesus alone has the words that give eternal life (zōēs aiōniou). The logic is relational and exclusionary: there is nowhere else to go, not because all alternatives are examined and rejected, but because the words here cannot be found elsewhere.
69: καὶ ἡμεῖς πεπιστεύκαμεν καὶ ἐγνώκαμεν — "and we have believed and have come to know." Both verbs are perfect tense: pepisteukamen (we have believed and our belief stands) and egnōkamen (we have come to know and that knowing stands). The order — first believe, then know — is the Johannine epistemology: faith precedes and grounds understanding. ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ ἅγιος τοῦ θεοῦ — "that you are the Holy One of God." Ho hagios tou theou — not "the Christ" (the Synoptic form of the confession at Caesarea Philippi) but "the Holy One of God," a title that marks consecration to and participation in the divine holiness. The title appears in Mark 1:24 in the mouth of a demon; Peter here uses it as acclamation.
70: οὐκ ἐγὼ ὑμᾶς τοὺς δώδεκα ἐξελεξάμην — "Did I not choose you, the Twelve?" The emphatic egō (I) stresses Jesus's own initiative in the election. Exelexamēn (aorist: I chose, a completed act). Despite the choosing, one of the Twelve is diabolos — not "a devil" in the sense of a fallen angel but the diabolic one, the slanderer/accuser. The article diabolos in Greek is the title given to Satan; its application to a human being is jarring. Jesus says he chose all twelve knowing what one of them would do (v.64 confirmed his foreknowledge).
71: ἔλεγεν δὲ τὸν Ἰούδαν Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτου — the Evangelist's parenthetical identification of Judas: "He was speaking of Judas son of Simon Iscariot." Iskariot is likely a geographic designation — from Kerioth in southern Judea — making Judas the only non-Galilean among the Twelve. The participle mellōn auton paradidōnai (about to betray him) uses the impersonal future participle: the betrayal is not yet accomplished but already in view. The word paradidōmi (G3860, to hand over, betray, deliver up) is the term used throughout the Passion narrative. Its introduction here, at the moment of the mass defection, places Judas's betrayal within the wider pattern of departures from Jesus that ch.6 has narrated.
